game_description: Eliminate characters by asking yes/no questions to find your opponent's pick.
filter_1_label: Opponent down to 5 or fewer characters?

strategies.strategy_detailed: In standard Guess Who, your card is dealt randomly — there is no selection. In variants where you choose: prefer rare features. Rare-feature characters are harder to isolate because binary-search questions rarely target rare attributes, giving opponent fewer yes-answer reveals.
strategy_stealth: Standard rules: card is dealt, no choice

strategies.strategy_detailed: Best first question splits the board near 12/12 (optimal binary search). In classic Guess Who, hair-related questions usually split well; gender questions are skewed (~19M/5F in the 1979 original — only ~8 elims/question). Avoid specific features early (mustache, hat) — they eliminate 2-3 only.

strategies.condition: Opponent answered yes to broad question
strategies.strategy_detailed: A 'yes' that leaves 10+ characters is still a good result if it eliminated 10+ others. Immediately follow with another 50/50 split of the remaining group. Never ask two consecutive questions about the same feature category — cover different attributes each turn.
strategy_stealth: Yes on broad Q = still eliminates half

strategies.condition: Opponent asks about a feature you have
strategies.strategy_detailed: Answer truthfully and instantly — hesitation telegraphs the answer before you say it. You can't see opponent's board (Hasbro design hides it), so track their narrowing via question pattern instead: broad early, specific late. Specific questions by turn 4 = they're at ~5 remaining.

If opponent is asking highly specific questions by turn 3, they've likely narrowed to a small group or are guessing. Accelerate your own elimination pace — ask a 50/50 now even if you planned to wait. First correct guess wins regardless of turn count.
strategy_stealth: Specific Q by turn 3 = they're close
First correct guess wins — race matters

strategies.strategy_detailed: At 8-12 characters, start asking about specific facial features: glasses, facial hair, teeth visible. These eliminate 2-5 characters per question but are now worth more since the group is smaller. Continue targeting the split closest to 50% of your remaining count.

Asking one more question then guessing wins ~75% (narrow to 1-2, then guess). Guessing now wins 33% at 3 chars, 25% at 4. Only race-guess when opponent is also at 3-4 and might guess first this round. Without that pressure, always ask — the odds favor patience by 2-3×.
strategy_stealth: Guess race only if opponent at 3-4

strategies.strategy_detailed: If you hold a rare-feature character and opponent directly asks about it, answer honestly — stalling is obvious. A direct question about your rare feature means they got lucky; most binary-search paths never hit it. Redirect fully to offense to finish before they adapt.
strategy_stealth: Rare feature directly asked = answer honestly
Direct rare Q = they got lucky
Redirect to offense — most paths miss it

strategies.condition: Two characters remain on your board
strategies.strategy_detailed: With 2 characters left, ask one more question only if you genuinely can't distinguish them yet. If you know which features separate them, ask the distinguishing question rather than guessing — a wrong guess hands the win to your opponent immediately.

strategies.condition: One character remains on your board
strategies.strategy_detailed: Guess immediately. Do not ask another question — asking costs a turn and your opponent might guess first. Say the name confidently. If you arrived here correctly, you win. If you made an error earlier, an extra question won't save you.

You can't prevent a correct guess — focus on making yours first. If opponent is at 2-3 characters and you're at 5+, they likely win unless you guess now. Take a calculated guess on the most probable candidate rather than playing perfectly and losing.
strategy_stealth: Can't prevent correct guess — beat them
Calculated guess at 5 if they're at 2
Perfect play loses if you're too slow
